Dose response curves show organism effects or mortality based on toxin or drug dosage.
Only approximately 10% of energy transfers between successive trophic levels.
Green Revolution introduced mechanization, GMOs, fertilizers, irrigation, and pesticides to boost food production.
Food webs model interlocking food chains showing energy and nutrient flow.
Biodiversity comprises genetic, species, and habitat diversity levels.
Clearcutting provides economic benefits but causes erosion, temperature increases, and flooding.
LD50 measures the chemical dose lethal to half a species population.
Primary treatment physically removes large objects and settles solid waste.
Recycling converts solid waste materials into new usable products.
Four ecosystem service categories: provisioning, regulating, cultural, and supporting.
Self-interested individuals overexploit shared resources, depleting them faster than sustainable levels.
Island biogeography studies ecological relationships and organism distribution on islands.
Biomass burning provides cheap energy but emits pollutants and causes deforestation.
Solid waste comprises discarded non-liquid, non-gas materials from various sectors.
Ecological tolerance is the range of environmental conditions organisms can survive.
Natural disruptions can have consequences equal to or exceeding human-made disruptions.
Selective absorption and concentration of elements in living organism cells, typically fat-soluble compounds.
POPs resist environmental degradation due to synthetic carbon-based molecular structure.
Heat released into water causes negative effects on aquatic organisms.
Eutrophication is nutrient enrichment of aquatic bodies of water.
Organisms adapt genetically to environments across short and long time scales.
Wetlands are ecosystems where water covers soil permanently or seasonally.
Primary and secondary succession represent two distinct types of ecological change.
Endocrine disruptors are chemicals interfering with animal endocrine systems.
Organisms tolerate pollutants within ranges; stress and death occur beyond optimum.
Point sources are single, identifiable pollution sources like smokestacks or pipes.
Specialists thrive in stable habitats; generalists succeed in changing environments.
K-selected species are large with few offspring, extended parental care, long lifespans.
Noise pollution causes physiological stress and hearing loss in organisms.
Acid rain results from nitrogen and sulfur oxides in atmosphere.
Air pollution reduction uses regulation, conservation, and alternative fuel sources.
Survivorship curves display relative survival rates of age cohorts from birth to maximum age.
El Niño and La Niña involve Pacific Ocean temperature changes affecting global climate patterns.
Environmental factors, particularly resources and space, limit population growth.
Sun's energy, mountains, and ocean temperature all influence weather and climate.
Carbon monoxide is an asphyxiant indoor air pollutant.
Natural CO2 sources include respiration, decomposition, and volcanic eruptions.
Fossil fuel combustion chemically reacts fuel with oxygen, producing CO2, water, and energy.
TFR depends on female age at first birth, education, family planning access, policy.
Nuclear fission of U-235 releases heat for electricity generation via turbines.
Thermal inversion reverses normal atmospheric temperature gradient, creating cooler surface air.
Multiple factors including birth/death rates and access to resources determine population growth.
Insolation varies by season and latitude as Earth's primary energy source.
Coal burning emits CO2, SO2, toxic metals, and particulate matter.
Demographic transition describes declining birth and death rates during industrialization using four-stage model.
Atmosphere comprises major gases in varying relative proportions.
Convergent boundaries create mountains, island arcs, earthquakes, volcanoes.
Home energy conservation includes thermostat adjustment, water conservation, efficient appliances, and landscaping.
Wind turbines convert kinetic energy of moving air into electricity.
Hydrogen fuel cells combine hydrogen and oxygen to produce electricity and water.
Soils develop through weathering, transport, and deposition of parent material.
Watersheds have distinct physical and biological characteristics including topography and vegetation.
Geothermal energy uses Earth's interior heat to generate electricity via steam.
NOx and VOCs react with heat and sunlight to form photochemical smog.
Soil water holding capacity varies by type, affecting productivity and fertility.
Depletion of accessible ores forces mining deeper, increasing resource use and pollution.
Hydroelectric power generated via dams, reservoirs, or turbines in flowing water.
Overfishing reduces fish populations, decreases aquatic biodiversity, and harms fishing-dependent communities.
Urbanization depletes resources and causes saltwater intrusion in water systems.
Ecological footprints measure resource demands and waste production per individual or society.
Sustainability means using resources without depleting them for future generations using key indicators.
Increase water infiltration using permeable pavement, trees, transit, vertical development.
Equatorial solar radiation, density differences, and Coriolis effect drive global winds.
IPM combines biological, physical, and limited chemical methods to control pests sustainably.
Meat production uses either CAFOs/feedlots or free-range grazing methods.
Pesticide use creates resistance through artificial selection but increases crop yields.
Soil conservation prevents erosion using contour plowing, windbreaks, perennials, terracing, no-till, and strip cropping.
Aquaculture expands due to efficiency, minimal water area, and low fuel requirements.
Photovoltaic cells convert sunlight to electricity; availability limits use.
Age structure diagram shapes reveal population growth rates.
Predators consume prey organisms in feeding relationships.
Deforestation mitigation uses reforestation, sustainable harvesting, and wood reuse.
HIPPCO framework identifies six primary causes of biodiversity decline.
Biomes contain plant and animal communities adapted to their climate.
Nonrenewable sources exist in fixed amounts and cannot be easily replaced.
Multiple factors threaten species: hunting, limited diet, invasive competition, habitat specificity.
Invasive species live outside their normal habitat, threatening native species.
Freshwater biomes provide essential drinking water resources.
Energy resource usage differs significantly between developed and developing nations.
Ocean acidification occurs from increased atmospheric CO2 via chemical processes.
Carbon cycle moves carbon atoms and molecules between sources and sinks.
Wood fuel, as firewood or charcoal, is accessible in developing nations.
Increased atmospheric greenhouse gases cause ocean warming.
Earth's climate has naturally fluctuated across geologic history via temperature shifts.
Nitrogen cycle involves movement of nitrogen atoms and molecules between sources and sinks.
Natural energy resources distribute unevenly globally based on geologic history.
Excess atmospheric greenhouse gases cause climate change, sea level rise, disease spread, and population shifts.
Five main greenhouse gases exist: CO₂, methane, water vapor, nitrous oxide, CFCs.
Phosphorus moves between sources and sinks through biogeochemical cycling.
Replace CFCs with substitutes like HFCs, though some increase greenhouse effect.
Sun-powered water cycle moves through solid, liquid, gaseous phases between sources and sinks.
Irrigation accounts for the majority of human freshwater consumption globally.
Primary productivity measures photosynthesis rate converting sunlight to organic compounds.
Tilling, slash-and-burn farming, and fertilizers damage the environment.
Stratospheric ozone layer essential for life evolution and survival.
Pathogens evolve to exploit new infection and transmission opportunities in populations.
Multiple chemical exposures complicate linking specific pollutants to human health effects.
Ecosystems require continuous energy input to sustain structure and matter transfer.
Burning fossil fuels heats water into steam, which turns turbines to generate electricity.
Symbiosis involves mutualism, commensalism, and parasitism interactions.
Nine major terrestrial biomes span from tundra to tropical rainforests.
Marine algae produce oxygen and absorb atmospheric carbon dioxide.
Carbon reservoirs hold compounds for varying durations, short or long.
Nitrogen compounds remain in most cycle reservoirs for relatively brief periods.
Rocks and sediments with phosphorus minerals serve as primary cycle reservoirs.
Oceans hold most water; ice caps and groundwater are minor water reservoirs.
Gross primary productivity is total photosynthesis rate in an area.
Biogeochemical cycles maintain life by conserving matter throughout ecosystems.
Thermodynamic laws explain energy loss between trophic levels.
Species removal or addition causes feedback effects throughout food web structure.
Greater genetic diversity improves stress response; bottlenecks reduce genetic diversity.
Human activities disrupt ecosystem services, causing economic and ecological consequences.
Islands were historically colonized by species arriving from other locations.
Tolerance ranges exist at both individual and species population levels.
Earth processes vary in timescale and occur periodically, episodically, or randomly.
Environmental changes force individuals to adapt behavior, relocate, or face extinction.
Keystone species disproportionately influence ecosystem structure and community composition.
r-selected species are small with many offspring, minimal parental care, short lifespans.
K-selected species show Type I or II curves; r-selected species show Type III curves.
Resources are finite and limited across all temporal scales.
Rapidly growing populations have proportionally more young people than stable ones.
Replacement-level fertility indicates relatively stable population.
Earth's carrying capacity and Malthusian factors limit global human population growth.
Developing countries exhibit higher infant mortality and greater child labor participation than developed nations.
Divergent boundaries produce seafloor spreading, rift valleys, volcanoes, earthquakes.
Soils are classified into horizons by composition and organic material content.
Particle size and composition influence soil porosity, permeability, and fertility.
Five atmosphere layers defined by temperature gradients: troposphere, stratosphere, mesosphere, thermosphere, exosphere.
Solar ray angle determines radiation intensity; perpendicular rays deliver maximum intensity.
Rain shadows form when mountains block precipitation from reaching downwind regions.
Geological and geographic factors influence El Niño and La Niña regional effects.
Trees absorb pollutants and store carbon; cutting releases carbon, accelerating climate change.
Farm mechanization increases profits and efficiency but heightens fossil fuel dependence.
Multiple irrigation methods exist: drip, flood, furrow, and spray systems.
Genetically engineered crops resist pests but reduce genetic diversity in crops.
Meat production requires twenty times more land than plant agriculture for equivalent calories.
Surface mining removes overburden and vegetation, increasing soil erosion susceptibility.
Urbanization increases atmospheric carbon dioxide via fossil fuel burning and landfills.
Sustainable yield is the harvest level that maintains renewable resource availability.
IPM reduces pesticide risks to wildlife, water supplies, and human health.
Soil fertility improves through crop rotation, green manure incorporation, and limestone application.
Aquaculture contaminates wastewater, escaped fish compete with wild populations, and high density spreads disease.
Forest protection from pests employs IPM and removal of infected trees.
Renewable sources replenish naturally at or near consumption rates and reuse.
Fossil fuels remain the dominant global energy source worldwide.
Peat is partially decomposed organic material used as combustible fuel.
Radioactive isotopes lose energy through radiation emission.
Ethanol fuel avoids net carbon emissions but has poor energy return on investment.
Active systems mechanically collect, heat, and store solar energy.
Tidal energy harnesses tidal flows to power turbines.
Geothermal energy has high access costs and limited accessibility; releases hydrogen sulfide.
Hydrogen fuel cells emit no carbon dioxide but require energy production and expensive technology.
Wind energy is renewable and clean but poses collision risks to birds and bats.
Large-scale energy conservation includes vehicle efficiency, electric vehicles, public transit, and green building design.
Fossil fuel combustion releases NOx causing ozone, smog, acid rain; also produces CO, hydrocarbons, particulates.
Multiple environmental factors influence photochemical smog formation rates.
Thermal inversions trap ground-level pollution including smog and particulate matter.
Multiple natural sources contribute particulate matter to the atmosphere.
Particulate indoor pollutants include asbestos, dust, and smoke.
Vapor recovery nozzles prevent fuel fumes release during vehicle refueling.
Motor vehicles and power plants emit nitrogen oxides; power plants emit sulfur dioxide.
Urban noise sources: transportation, construction, domestic and industrial activities.
Nonpoint sources are diffused, hard-to-identify pollution like pesticides or runoff.
Coral reefs damaged by warming, sediment, and overfishing.
Endocrine disruptors cause birth defects, developmental disorders, gender imbalances.
Wetlands provide essential services: water purification, flood protection, filtration, habitat.
Algal blooms from excess nutrients consume oxygen upon decomposition, causing hypoxia and organism death.
Warmer water holds less dissolved oxygen than colder water.
Fat-soluble POPs accumulate in organism tissues, causing toxicity through bioaccumulation.
Substance concentration increases in body tissue at progressively higher trophic levels.
Landfills are primary disposal method but cause groundwater contamination and gas emissions.
Recycling reduces mineral demand but requires significant energy and money.
Secondary treatment uses aerated bacteria to decompose organic matter into CO₂ and sludge.
Untreated sewage in waterways causes dysentery infection in humans.
Pathogens persist across diverse environments despite apparently clean sanitary conditions.
Ozone depletion caused by CFCs and natural atmospheric ice melting.
Water vapor's short atmospheric residence time limits its climate change contribution.
Climate change causes warming, ice melt, sea level rise, and human displacement.
Ocean warming affects marine species through habitat loss and physiological changes.
Oceans absorb atmospheric CO2, becoming progressively more acidic.
Invasive species are generalists that outcompete native species for resources.
Adaptable species and those able to relocate face lower extinction risk.
Roads, agriculture, and logging break habitats into isolated fragments.
Carbon cycles between photosynthesis and cellular respiration in organisms.
Oil spills kill marine life through toxicity, coating, and seabed contamination.
Greenhouse effect maintains Earth's surface temperature required for life.
Tertiary treatment removes remaining pollutants via ecological or chemical processes.
Multiple human intervention strategies can control invasive species populations.
Composting decomposes organic waste into fertilizer; drawbacks include odor and rodents.
E-waste consists of discarded televisions, cell phones, computers, and similar devices.
Island species evolve as specialists due to limited resources; invasive generalists threaten their survival.
Persistent biomagnified substances cause eggshell thinning and developmental deformities in top carnivores.
Indicator species reveal distinctive characteristics and quality of ecosystems through presence or abundance.
Competition for limited resources; partitioning reduces negative impacts.
Climate has changed throughout Earth's history due to multiple causes.
Warming causes coral bleaching when algae loss turns corals white; outcomes vary.
Development, dams, overfishing, and agricultural/industrial pollution threaten wetlands.
POPs disperse globally through atmospheric and aquatic transport mechanisms.
Marine resource distribution depends on salinity, depth, turbidity, nutrients, temperature.
Hypoxic waterways contain low dissolved oxygen levels.
Sea level changes affect marine ecosystems both positively and negatively.
Human activities causing ocean acidification include fossil fuel burning and deforestation.
Burning diesel fuels releases sulfur dioxide, degrading air quality.
Hydroelectric power produces no air pollution but causes habitat loss and expensive construction.
Density-independent and density-dependent factors both influence human population growth rates.
Wind and water erode soils; soil protection maintains water quality through filtration.
NOx produced early; ozone peaks afternoon and summer due to oxygen-sunlight reactions.
Passive systems absorb heat directly without equipment or storage.
Multiple extraction methods retrieve fossil fuels from Earth for energy production.
Excess soil water raises groundwater tables and prevents root oxygen absorption.
Three coal types form from heat, pressure, and burial depth variations.
Maternal healthcare and nutrition access determine infant mortality rates.
Less ozone increases UV rays, causing skin cancer and cataracts.
Lack of atmospheric phosphorus makes it naturally scarce and limiting.
Developing nations increase fossil fuel dependence as they industrialize.
Abundant resources typically cause population growth to accelerate.
Selective pressures alter organism behaviors and fitness in their environment.
Indoor pollutants originate from natural, human-made, and combustion sources.
Nitrogen fixation converts atmospheric nitrogen to ammonia for plant uptake and tissue synthesis.
Net primary productivity measures energy storage after respiratory losses.
Prescribed burns reduce natural wildfire occurrence through controlled fire management.
Rotational grazing rotates livestock between pastures to prevent localized overgrazing damage.
IPM minimizes environmental disruption and health threats but involves complexity and cost.
CAFOs enable rapid livestock production but create crowded conditions, waste contamination, and lower consumer costs.
Impervious surfaces prevent water infiltration, causing flooding in urban areas.
Mining produces slag and tailings waste while providing low-cost energy; coal mining harms habitats and water.
Catalytic converters transform engine exhaust pollutants into less harmful compounds.
Various testing methods assess soil properties to inform irrigation and fertilization decisions.
Climate zone expansion spreads pathogens and vectors into previously unaffected subtropical and temperate regions.
U-235's long radioactivity creates nuclear waste disposal challenges.
Different species experience varying impacts from same habitat fragmentation.
Acid deposition impacts downwind communities from coal-burning power plants.
Transform boundaries cause earthquakes.
Asbestos exposure primarily causes mesothelioma, a serious cancer type.
Biotic potential is maximum reproductive rate under ideal environmental conditions.
Resource distribution varies with climate, geography, latitude, altitude, nutrients, soil.
Noise pollution stresses animals, masks communication, damages hearing, alters migration.
Solar energy flows through trophic levels from producers to higher consumers.
Higher species numbers increase ecosystem resilience to disturbances.
Equatorial regions receive highest solar radiation; intensity decreases poleward.
Soil texture triangles classify soils by clay, silt, and sand percentages.
Atmosphere serves as nitrogen cycle's primary reservoir.
Resource competition for territory, food, mates, habitat endangers species.
Solar is clean and low-impact but expensive; farms harm deserts.
CO₂ (GWP=1) is reference; CFCs > N₂O > methane in warming potency.
Plate boundary maps predict volcano, earthquake, hot spot, and fault locations.
Decomposition stored carbon for millions of years; fossil fuel burning releases it rapidly.
The rule of 70 calculates population doubling time from growth rate percentage.
EPA Clean Air Act regulations on fuel lead reduced atmospheric lead dramatically.
Atmospheric circulation distributes heat; climate change may alter circulation patterns.
VOCs evaporate at room temperature; examples include formaldehyde, gasoline, tree emissions.
Shrinking resources cause unequal distribution, mortality, reduced fecundity, declining growth.
Natural indoor pollutants include radon, mold, and dust.
Scrubbers remove particulates and gases from industrial exhaust streams.
Many species exhibit mixed or conditional reproductive strategies between r and K selection.
Acid deposition acidifies soils, water, and corrodes human-made structures.
Pioneer species colonize unoccupied habitats and adapt, potentially leading to new species formation.
Beach oil spills damage fishing and tourism economies.
Oligotrophic waterways have low nutrients, stable algae, and high dissolved oxygen.
Sea level fluctuations result from variations in glacial ice throughout geological time.
Acidification impairs coral shell formation by reducing available calcium carbonate.
Biomagnification harms human reproductive, nervous, and circulatory systems.
Biome distribution is dynamic and shifts with global climate change.
Sanitary landfills contain liners, water systems, leachate collection, caps, and methane systems.
Climate change eliminates habitats through temperature, precipitation, and sea level shifts.
E-waste recycling prevents hazardous metals from leaching into groundwater.
Habitat loss eliminates specialists, then generalists, and reduces large-range species.
Final disinfection with chlorine, ozone, or UV light kills remaining bacteria.
High tropospheric ozone levels harm respiratory health and lung function.
Productivity is measured in energy per area per time units.
Poor sanitation and contaminated water in low-income areas facilitate infectious disease spread.
Declining accessible coal reserves necessitate expensive subsurface mining operations.
Urban sprawl expands low-density suburbs into rural lands, creating environmental problems.
Free-range grazing produces chemical-free meat with natural fertilizer but requires large land and higher costs.
Global industrialization drives increased energy demand worldwide.
Furrow irrigation is cheap but loses approximately one-third water to evaporation.
Natural gas, the cleanest fossil fuel, consists primarily of methane.
Seasonal solar radiation peaks at summer solstice, minimums at winter solstice.
Fracking contaminates groundwater and releases volatile organic compounds into environment.
Nuclear is nonrenewable, clean regarding air but produces thermal and solid waste pollution.
Ecological succession alters biomass, species richness, and productivity in disturbed ecosystems.
Major nuclear accidents caused environmental impacts through radioactive release.
Earthquakes result from stress overcoming locked faults.
Species richness measures the count of different species in ecosystems.
DDT, mercury, and PCBs bioaccumulate with significant environmental impacts.
Resource availability, cost, and policy determine energy source choices.
Protection strategies: criminalize poaching, protect habitats, enact legislation.
Crude oil from tar sands combines clay, sand, water, and bitumen.
Air pollutants are classified as primary or secondary types.
Water light penetration limits aquatic photosynthesis; organisms adapted accordingly.
Landfill decomposition depends on trash composition and conditions for microbial activity.
Agricultural runoff and wastewater release are human causes of eutrophication.
Limestone bedrock neutralizes acid rain effects; regional geology varies resilience.
Landfill strategies include energy recovery and habitat restoration for parks.
Human-made pollutants include insulation, VOCs, formaldehyde, and lead.
Domesticated species management reduces genetic biodiversity of those organisms.
Coal power plants use scrubbers and electrostatic precipitators for pollution control.
Axial tilt causes seasonal variation and daylight hour changes at each location.
Ocean currents distribute heat globally; changes significantly impact coastal climates.
Flood irrigation loses twenty percent to evaporation and risks soil waterlogging.
Nutrient pollution creates low-oxygen dead zones in oceans.
Plague bacteria spread to humans through infected organism bites or contaminated fluid contact.
K-selected species vulnerable to invasive species; most invasives are r-selected.
Photochemical smog commonly forms in urban areas due to motor vehicle emissions.
Major environmental changes cause widespread habitat alterations across large areas.
Overgrazing removes vegetation and causes soil erosion through excessive animal feeding.
Combustion pollutants include CO, NOx, SO2, particulates, and tobacco smoke.
Protected areas, corridors, sustainable practices, and restoration mitigate biodiversity loss.
Climate change impacts soil viability and increases erosion through temperature and rainfall shifts.
Fossil fuels are refined into specialized fuels for specific applications.
Incineration reduces waste volume but releases significant air pollutants into atmosphere.
Reducing NOx and VOC emissions decreases photochemical smog formation.
Half-life enables calculation of decay rates and radioactivity levels over time.
Landfill gas combustion generates electricity and reduces landfill volume.
Overgrazing degrades arid regions into deserts through progressive environmental degradation.
Tuberculosis spreads via inhalation of bacteria from infected person bodily fluids.
Wildlife migrates short and long-term for various reasons including natural disruptions.
Oxygen sag curves show dissolved oxygen decline from pollution sources.
Spray irrigation is efficient but expensive and energy-intensive compared to alternatives.
Industrial heavy metals contaminate groundwater and drinking supplies.
Cogeneration uses fuel to simultaneously produce heat and electricity.
Reduced meat consumption decreases emissions, water use, antibiotics, hormones, and improves soil.
Malaria, a parasitic disease from infected mosquito bites, predominantly occurs in sub-Saharan Africa.
Radon-222 is radioactive gas from uranium decay in rocks.
Drip irrigation is most efficient but prohibitively expensive for widespread adoption.
Polar regions respond faster to climate change due to ice-albedo positive feedback.
Illegal disposal of rejected items like tires creates environmental hazards and disease vectors.
Photochemical smog causes respiratory problems and eye irritation in humans.
Salt accumulation from irrigation water evaporation eventually makes soil toxic.
West Nile virus transmits to humans through infected mosquito bites.
Radon enters homes through soil, basements, cracks, and groundwater.
Aquatic litter harms wildlife and introduces toxins into food chains.
Melting polar ice absorbs more solar energy, amplifying regional warming.
Ocean dumping creates plastic islands, causing wildlife entanglement and ingestion problems.
Radon exposure causes lung cancer, America's second leading cancer cause.
Arctic warming accelerates through feedback loops releasing methane from thawing tundra.
Irrigation overuse depletes aquifers, exemplified by the Ogallala Aquifer depletion.
SARS pneumonia spreads through inhalation or contact with infected respiratory fluids.
Excess sediment reduces light, harming producers and predators.
MERS is a viral respiratory illness transmitted from animals to humans.
Bacteria convert elemental mercury to highly toxic methylmercury in water.
Polar ice loss threatens species dependent on ice for habitat and food.
Zika spreads via infected mosquito bites and sexual contact between humans.
Cholera, a bacterial disease, is contracted through consumption of infected water.
